feed.title: Combined summary - Babilonia - probabilistic coinjoin and covert betting

entry.title: Combined summary - Babilonia - probabilistic coinjoin and covert betting
entry.summary: A new protocol inspired by trustless onchain coin-flips has been developed, providing notable advancements in privacy and efficiency for cryptocurrency transactions. This protocol is distinguished by its minimal onchain footprint, requiring only two small transaction sizes and typically one confirmation, though occasionally a third might be needed. It offers a system that appears as ordinary payments or payjoins to outside observers, thereby supporting invisible fair betting between two parties. At the heart of the protocol lies its implementation of simple Zero-Knowledge Proof (ZKP) techniques which do not depend on complex cryptographic processes. The mechanism operates similarly to payjoins but can be perceived as involving two such operations depending on interpretation. A unique feature introduced is "pot sweeteners," which enables incentives between parties without making transactions identifiable, thus enhancing transaction privacy beyond traditional methods like those used in Joinmarket. The protocol also incorporates decoys within BIP324 traffic, enabling nodes to negotiate privately and counteract Sybil attacks effectively. The codebase, primarily developed using LLM coding with minimal human intervention, is available for community review and testing [here](https://github.com/AdamISZ/babilonia). Although existing documentation requires further refinement for wider accessibility, future updates are anticipated based on community feedback. Practical applications of this protocol include swaps, showcasing improved features albeit with some trade-offs. Detailed exploration of these capabilities and underlying theories can be accessed through the [Babilonia paper](https://github.com/AdamISZ/babilonia-paper) and example transactions on the Signet test network [here](https://mempool.space/signet/tx/59bb7633314b35d2edcff7997281c0254e9a9b36faf22dbc643d4a319d92f064) and [here](https://mempool.space/signet/tx/1112032da666df1efaae5c2e8d567bfa200e0a94dd1c5aa8e1e89a14b32f4c44). Further insights reveal a comparison of this protocol with GTT 26, another cryptographic construction discussed in academic circles. GTT 26 employs an OPRF that allows for very low probabilities and supports complex structures like lotteries. In contrast, the simpler design of the Babilonia protocol permits straightforward transaction flows akin to coinjoin or payjoin, maintaining a more limited but practical betting function. This simplicity facilitates easier integration and operation, avoiding the complexities associated with setups that require secretive key management and blind evaluation requests. When considering the application of the Babilonia protocol to coinswaps rather than coinjoins, it introduces a method to enhance privacy and reduce amount correlation significantly. By creating output transactions with variable amounts negotiated in private, it achieves a fair and statistically sound distribution of funds between parties involved in a swap. This approach addresses the inherent limitations of amount correlation in coinswaps, presenting a potentially powerful solution for enhancing the effectiveness and security of such transactions.
